name: McCain Enterprise AKS Platform — Private Multi-Tenant Kubernetes
strategicIntent: Deploy a single, private, enterprise-grade Azure Kubernetes Service cluster within the McCain Azure landing zone to provide a governed, multi-tenant container platform that eliminates per-team cluster sprawl while maintaining strong workload isolation boundaries. The pattern enforces Zero Trust principles through least-privilege identity, namespace-level network segmentation, and software supply-chain integrity controls embedded as non-negotiable platform guardrails rather than optional consumer choices. It directly supports the McCain architecture pillars of Security by Design, Cost Accountability, and Operational Excellence by codifying shared platform services, structured chargeback, and standardized observability into the foundational cluster design.
problemStatement: McCain requires a scalable Kubernetes platform that can host workloads from multiple Business Units on shared infrastructure without compromising security isolation or operational governance. Running a public AKS cluster exposes the API server to the internet and increases the blast radius of any compromise. Without a structured multi-tenant model, BU workloads can interfere with each other, costs cannot be attributed accurately, and policy enforcement is inconsistent across teams.

useCases.use: Hosting containerized application workloads for multiple McCain Business Units that require strong namespace-level isolation, centralized ingress, and audit-grade policy enforcement on a shared AKS cluster.
useCases.dontUse: Workloads that require dedicated clusters for compliance or data-sovereignty reasons, or OT/industrial edge workloads that cannot route through the enterprise hub network.

useCases.use: Teams adopting GitOps-based progressive delivery (Argo CD) with workload identity for secretless pod-to-Azure-service authentication.
useCases.dontUse: Workloads that need Windows node pools, GPU-accelerated compute, or highly privileged host-level access that violates the PSS-Restricted policy baseline enforced on this cluster.

useCases.use: Internet-facing or internal applications that require centralized L7 ingress with WAF, TLS termination from Azure Key Vault, and outbound egress filtering via Azure Firewall.
useCases.dontUse: Standalone single-tenant applications where a dedicated AKS cluster or Azure App Service would be more cost-effective and operationally simpler.

components: Private AKS Cluster — API server exposed only via Azure Private Link; no public endpoint; reachable through hub VPN/ExpressRoute/Zscaler and approved jump hosts.
System Node Pool — Hosts critical AKS add-ons with taint CriticalAddonsOnly=true to prevent BU workload scheduling on system nodes.
User Node Pools (per BU) — Dedicated VMSS-backed node pools with taints, labels, and Cluster Autoscaler (min/max) for BU workload isolation and independent scaling.
Azure CNI Overlay Networking — Pod CIDR 10.244.0.0/16 decoupled from the VNet address space, enabling IP efficiency and scale within spoke VNet 10.125.72.0/21.
Application Gateway v2 — Centralized L7 ingress with public and private frontends; WAF policies delegated to Cloudflare upstream; routes to AKS Internal Load Balancer.
Managed NGINX Ingress (App Routing Add-on) — Internal Load Balancer-backed ingress controllers handling TLS termination using certificates fetched from Azure Key Vault via workload identity.
Cloudflare WAF — Edge WAF and DNS proxy for internet-facing applications; primary WAF tier per McCain standard before traffic reaches Application Gateway.
Azure Firewall — Centralized egress control enforcing required FQDNs and service tags for AKS node outbound traffic; hub-hosted.
Azure Container Registry (Premium) — Private container image registry with private endpoint, content trust, and Defender-integrated vulnerability scanning.
Azure Key Vault + Secrets Store CSI Driver — Secrets, certificates, and keys management with private endpoint, RBAC, purge protection, and autorotation mounted into pods.
Workload Identity (OIDC) — Federated identity per Kubernetes service account for secretless pod authentication to Azure services; no cluster-scoped static secrets.
Microsoft Entra ID + Azure RBAC — Cluster authentication and authorization; local Kubernetes accounts disabled; least-privilege RBAC enforced.

Cilium Network Policy (v1.17.9) — Managed AKS network policy add-on enforcing deny-by-default tenant isolation via CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy (platform-owned) and CiliumNetworkPolicy (BU-scoped), managed via Argo CD with drift detection.
Azure Policy Add-on (OPA/Gatekeeper) — Enforces PSS-Restricted pod security standards, supply chain controls, network access rules, and platform governance policies at admission time.
Defender for Containers — Runtime threat protection and vulnerability assessment for cluster workloads and ACR images.
Notation v2 + Ratify — Container image signing in the CI/CD pipeline (GitHub DevSecOps); signature verification enforced at admission via Ratify integrated with Gatekeeper (currently audit mode).
Argo CD (GitOps Controller) — Progressive delivery and GitOps reconciliation integrated with Entra ID for team-scoped log and application access; manages Cilium policy lifecycle.
Observability Integration — AKS metrics and logs integrated with McCain Observability solution (Grafana/Log Analytics); managed by IE&O observability team.
Azure DevOps Pipelines + Terraform — Infrastructure-as-code automation for cluster provisioning with linting, policy checks, image scanning, and GitOps integration.
interactionSteps: Step 1 — Internal Traffic Ingress: A client on the McCain network resolves app-int.mccain.com via the internal Domain Controller DNS to the Application Gateway private frontend IP.
Step 2 — TLS Handshake to App Gateway: The client initiates an HTTPS (443) TLS handshake with the Application Gateway private listener.
Step 3 — App Gateway to AKS ILB: Application Gateway forwards the request over HTTPS (443) to the Internal Load Balancer IP associated with the managed NGINX ingress controller.
Step 4 — Certificate Fetch: The webapprouting managed identity authenticates via workload identity (OIDC) to Azure Key Vault to retrieve the TLS certificate for SSL offloading.
Step 5 — ILB to NGINX Ingress: The Internal Load Balancer distributes traffic across available NGINX ingress controller pods.
Step 6 — NGINX to Application Pod: NGINX performs SSL offloading and routes plain HTTP traffic through Azure CNI Overlay to the target BU application pod on its dedicated node pool.

Step 7 — Internet Traffic Ingress: An internet client resolves the public domain via Cloudflare DNS; Cloudflare WAF inspects and proxies traffic to the Application Gateway public frontend IP over HTTPS (443).

Step 8 — App Gateway Routes Internet Traffic: Application Gateway forwards the Cloudflare-proxied request to the same AKS Internal Load Balancer backend, following the same steps 4–6 as internal traffic.

Step 9 — Outbound Egress: AKS node and pod egress traffic is routed through Azure Firewall in the hub, which enforces allowed FQDNs and service tags (e.g., mcr.microsoft.com, management.azure.com, login.microsoftonline.com, vault.azure.net) before permitting outbound internet connectivity.

Step 10 — Policy Admission: On every pod deployment, Azure Policy (Gatekeeper) evaluates PSS-Restricted controls and supply chain policies; Ratify verifies Notation v2 image signatures against ACR before admission is granted.

Step 11 — GitOps Reconciliation: Argo CD continuously reconciles cluster state against the mccain-aks-platform Git repository; Cilium CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy drift is auto-remediated; BU CiliumNetworkPolicies are deployed per tenant namespace via ApplicationSet.

Step 12 — Namespace Onboarding: New BU tenants are onboarded by labeling their namespace with platform.io/tenant=true, creating an Entra ID security group mapped to an Argo CD RBAC role, provisioning a dedicated user node pool, and applying BU-scoped Cilium network policies.
securityConsiderations: The AKS API server is private with no public endpoint; all control plane access must traverse the hub network via ExpressRoute, VPN, Zscaler, or approved jump hosts — any deviation requires an explicit exception.
Kubernetes local accounts are disabled cluster-wide; all human and service principal authentication must use Microsoft Entra ID with Azure RBAC and least-privilege role assignments.
Workload Identity (OIDC) is mandatory for pod-to-Azure-service authentication; static credentials or cluster-scoped Kubernetes secrets for Azure resource access are not permitted.

Cilium network policies enforce deny-by-default isolation per tenant namespace; intra-namespace traffic is allowed by default, but cross-namespace and external egress must be explicitly permitted via platform-approved policies.

All container images must be sourced exclusively from the private ACR with private endpoints; images must be signed with Notation v2 and verified by Ratify at admission (currently audit mode, to be enforced).

PSS-Restricted pod security policies are enforced at admission: no privileged containers, no host namespace sharing, read-only root filesystems, restricted capabilities, and approved AppArmor profiles.
Outbound egress from AKS nodes is restricted to Azure Firewall with an approved FQDN and service tag allowlist; unrestricted internet egress from pods is not permitted.
Secrets, certificates, and keys must be stored in Azure Key Vault with private endpoints, RBAC authorization, purge protection, and soft delete enabled; autorotation via CSI driver is required.
Defender for Containers must remain enabled for runtime threat detection, vulnerability assessment of running containers, and ACR image scanning.
Cloudflare WAF is the primary edge WAF for all internet-facing applications; Application Gateway WAF policies serve as a secondary layer and must not be disabled.
Argo CD access is scoped per BU via Entra ID group-to-RBAC-role mapping; teams may only access logs and resources within their own namespaces.
TLS must be enforced for all ingress traffic; TLS certificates are managed exclusively through Azure Key Vault and fetched by the webapprouting managed identity.

guardrails.rule: AKS API server must remain private with no public endpoint enabled; local Kubernetes accounts must be disabled; all access via Entra ID with Azure RBAC.
All workloads must comply with PSS-Restricted standards: no privileged containers, no privilege escalation, read-only root filesystem, no host namespace sharing, and only approved capabilities.
Pod-to-Azure authentication must use OIDC Workload Identity with per-service-account federated identities; static credentials stored as Kubernetes secrets for Azure resource access are prohibited.

Container images must be pulled exclusively from the private ACR, signed with Notation v2, and verified by Ratify at admission; images with unresolved critical vulnerabilities (MDVM) must not be deployed to production.

All tenant namespaces must carry the label platform.io/tenant=true and be subject to Cilium deny-by-default network policies; cross-namespace traffic requires an explicit platform-approved CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy.
All AKS node and pod outbound traffic must route through Azure Firewall in the hub; direct internet egress bypassing the firewall is not permitted.
Application teams must deploy with a minimum of 2 replicas, define PodDisruptionBudgets, configure readiness and liveness probes, and use pod anti-affinity rules to distribute across nodes.
Cluster upgrades must occur within approved maintenance windows; control plane is upgraded before node pools; node pools are upgraded sequentially with maxSurge set to 33%.

All secrets and certificates must be stored in Azure Key Vault with private endpoints, RBAC authorization model, purge protection, and soft delete; mounted into pods via Secrets Store CSI with autorotation.
Workloads must not run in the default Kubernetes namespace; each BU must operate within a dedicated namespace with appropriate RBAC, resource limits, and Cilium policies applied at onboarding.
Resource logs for AKS must be enabled and integrated with the McCain Observability solution (Log Analytics/Grafana); Defender for Containers must remain enabled on all clusters.

All AKS resources must be tagged with BU, application, and environment tags to enable chargeback calculation; BU cost is computed as shared platform cost divided by onboarded BU count plus dedicated VMSS node pool cost.

CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicies are owned exclusively by the platform team and managed via Argo CD with drift detection and auto-remediation enabled; BU teams may not directly modify cluster-wide policies.
Azure Container Registry must use Premium SKU with private endpoints only; public network access to ACR must be disabled; geo-replication is recommended for DR scenarios.

exceptionProcess: Any exception to the guardrails defined in this pattern (e.g., enabling public AKS API endpoint, allowing privileged containers, bypassing image signing verification, or permitting direct internet egress) must be submitted to the CCoE and Platform Engineering team with documented business justification, compensating controls, and a remediation timeline. Exceptions are reviewed on a case-by-case basis and, if approved, are time-bound with mandatory re-review at the next quarterly architecture governance cycle. Unapproved deviations detected via Azure Policy or Argo CD drift detection will trigger an automated alert to the Platform Engineering team for immediate remediation.

revisionHistory.author: AI Assistant
revisionHistory.summary: Initial APL entry derived from McCain Enterprise AKS Platform TDD v1.2 (Feb–Mar 2026), covering private cluster design, multi-tenant networking, security controls, image signing, Cilium policies, upgrade strategy, and chargeback model.
